summary:
Rakhigarhi, located in present-day Haryana, India, is one of the largest sites of the [[event:1680|Indus Valley Civilization]], with occupation layers dating back to the early Harappan period around 4600 BCE. By the mature Harappan phase (approximately 2600–1900 BCE), Rakhigarhi had grown to cover an estimated 80 hectares or more, making it comparable in size to or larger than Mohenjo-daro, though systematic excavation of the full site remains incomplete.

Archaeological work at Rakhigarhi has uncovered planned street grids, brick-built drains, granaries, and workshop areas consistent with the urban planning conventions found at other Indus sites. Ancient DNA analysis of a skeletal burial from Rakhigarhi, published in 2019, contributed to scholarly debate about the genetic origins of the Harappan population and its relationship to later South Asian populations. The site's size and early occupation make it a critical reference point for understanding the origins and geographic extent of the Indus Valley Civilization.

read_more_links.title: Harappan Sites of India - Archaeological Survey of India
